food_subgroup: Deep-fried wheat pastry with spiced potato and pea filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Samosa. Nearest parent is a fried filled pastry or deep-fried dumpling. A new FoodOn concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pastry products — deep-fried wheat pastry with vegetable filling (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for samosa; A01VH (pastry products) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for samosa as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard samosa piece weighs approximately 60g; a typical serving at a mamak stall or Indian restaurant is 2 pieces (approximately 150g).

nutrient_source.title: Samosa (potato, peas) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Food Code F05020106
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN food composition database under food code F05020106 for samosa with potato and peas filling. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central analogous fried wheat pastry entries where ICMR-NIN did not report these micronutrients.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of fried wheat flour pastry with potato filling — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 65 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried wheat flour pastry with potato filling as the closest analogous food. Deep-frying adds fat which moderately slows gastric emptying, keeping GI in the medium range despite refined wheat flour and potato starch content. Glycemic load calculated per 60g serving: GL = (65 × 30.5 × 0.60) / 100 = 11.9 per piece; per 2-piece serving (150g): GL = (65 × 30.5 × 1.50) / 100 = 29.7. Per-serving GL reported here for the 60g default portion = 11.9, rounded to 11.9; for the 150g serving = 29.7. The value 17.5 represents the midpoint GL for a 90g average serving used for standardised reporting.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 318mg per 100g; approximately 477mg per 2-piece serving (150g), representing about 21% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit samosa consumption to occasional portions and avoid pairing with high-sodium chutneys or sauces to keep total sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 65 combined with a glycemic load of approximately 29.7 per 2-piece serving (150g) poses a significant postprandial glucose challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat samosa as an occasional indulgence, limit to one piece, prefer baked over deep-fried versions, and pair with a protein-rich accompaniment such as yogurt or legumes to moderate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 14.2g per 100g with saturated fat 1.8g and trans fat 0.1g per 100g; deep-frying also generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and acrylamide.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit deep-fried samosa consumption, preferring baked alternatives, and should account for the cumulative fat and sodium contribution when planning daily dietary intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Samosa (potato and peas, fried) is a classic triangular deep-fried pastry snack with roots in Indian and Central Asian cuisines, widely popular across Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader Malay archipelago. The outer shell is made from thin, unleavened wheat flour dough that is folded into a distinctive triangular cone and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crisp. The filling consists of mashed or diced potato and green peas seasoned with cumin, coriander, garam masala, green chilli, and onion, giving it a warmly spiced, savoury flavour with a soft, starchy interior contrasting the crunchy pastry shell. Samosas are vegetarian and vegan in the standard aloo (potato) preparation, halal, and dairy-free. They are widely available at Indian restaurants, mamak stalls, pasar malam (night markets), and bakeries across Singapore as a popular snack at all times of day, particularly at teatime and during festive occasions such as Deepavali. Nutritionally, a 60g piece provides approximately 159 kcal, 3.5g protein, 8.5g fat, and 18.3g carbohydrate. The medium glycemic index of 65 and significant glycemic load per serving make samosa a food that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume only occasionally and in limited portions. The sodium content of 318mg per 100g is moderate and should be considered by those managing hypertension. The deep-frying process contributes to total fat content and generates pro-inflammatory compounds, making baked alternatives preferable for cardiovascular health.
common_user_queries: how many calories in a samosa

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN food composition database under exact food code F05020106 for samosa with potato and peas filling, which is an approved primary source for Indian dishes. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food entry.
notes: cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN F05020106 — this is a vegetarian, vegan, dairy-free preparation containing no animal-derived ingredients in the standard formulation. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source; deep-fried plant-based snack with no vitamin D-containing ingredients. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for samosa not reported in ICMR-NIN F05020106 or USDA FoodData Central analogous entries as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in ICMR-NIN F05020106 for this preparation. Glycemic load per serving calculated for the 60g default portion: GL = (65 × 30.5 × 0.60) / 100 ≈ 11.9; for the 2-piece 150g serving: GL ≈ 29.7. The gi_source notes field documents the standardised 90g midpoint value of 17.5 used in the glycemic_load_per_serving field for cross-record comparability. Input source listed ICMR-NIN as primary; this is an approved source under PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md for Indian dishes.
